title: Pronouns & Verb Agreement
situation: Master who does what — how pronouns control verb forms in Hindi

phrases.hindi: main / tu / tum / aap / woh / hum
phrases.english: I / you(intimate) / you(casual) / you(formal) / he-she-they / we
phrases.context: Hindi has 3 levels of 'you': tu (intimate), tum (friendly), aap (respectful). Using wrong one = social disaster
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN / TU / TUM / AAP / WOH / HUM

phrases.hindi: main jaata hoon, tu jaata hai, tum jaate ho, aap jaate hain
phrases.english: I go, you(int) go, you(cas) go, you(formal) go
phrases.context: Same verb 'jaana' changes helper: hoon/hai/ho/hain. The verb ending (-ta/-te) also shifts for tum/aap
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN jaa-TA hoon, TU jaa-TA hai, TUM jaa-TE ho, AAP jaa-TE hain

phrases.hindi: mujhe / tujhe / tumhe / aapko / usko / humko
phrases.english: to me / to you(int) / to you(cas) / to you(formal) / to him-her / to us
phrases.context: Object forms — used when something happens TO someone. 'mujhe bhookh lagi' = I'm hungry (hunger struck to-me)
phrases.pronunciation: MUJH-e / TUJH-e / TUM-he / AAP-ko / US-ko / HUM-ko

phrases.hindi: mera / tera / tumhara / aapka / uska / hamara
phrases.english: my / your(int) / your(cas) / your(formal) / his-her / our
phrases.context: Possessives — these also change gender! mera (my-masc), meri (my-fem), mere (my-plural). Same pattern for all
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ra / TE-ra / tum-HA-ra / AAP-ka / US-ka / ha-MA-ra

phrases.hindi: meri behen / mera bhai / mere dost
phrases.english: my sister / my brother / my friends
phrases.context: Possessive matches the OWNED thing's gender: meri (fem noun), mera (masc noun), mere (plural noun)
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ri be-HEN / ME-ra BHAI / ME-re DOST

phrases.hindi: woh jaata hai / woh jaati hai / woh jaate hain
phrases.english: He goes / She goes / They go
phrases.context: 'woh' means he/she/it/they! Same word. Verb form tells you: jaata=he, jaati=she, jaate hain=they
phrases.pronunciation: WOH jaa-TA hai / WOH jaa-TI hai / WOH jaa-TE hain

phrases.hindi: aap baitho / tum baitho / tu baith
phrases.context: Commands change by pronoun: aap/tum get '-o' or '-iye' (polite), tu gets bare root. 'aap baithiye' = very polite
phrases.pronunciation: AAP BAITH-o / TUM BAITH-o / TU BAITH

phrases.hindi: mujhe lagta hai ki woh nahi aayega
phrases.english: I think (it seems to me) that he won't come
phrases.context: 'mujhe lagta hai' = it seems to me / I think. Literally 'to-me it-seems is'. Indirect construction — very natural in Hindi
phrases.pronunciation: MUJH-e LAG-ta hai ki WOH na-HI aa-YE-ga

phrases.english: I need to ask you something
phrases.context: 'tumse' = from you (tum + se). Pronoun + postposition forms: mujhse (from me), tumse (from you), usse (from him/her)

phrases.hindi: apna kaam karo, mera kaam mujhe karne do
phrases.english: Do your own work, let me do my work
phrases.context: 'apna' = one's own (reflexive). Used when subject and possessor are same person. 'Main apna kaam karta hoon' = I do my own work
phrases.pronunciation: AP-na KAAM ka-RO, ME-ra KAAM MUJH-e kar-NE do

grammar_notes: Three 'you' levels: tu (intimate — lovers, God, very close friends, anger), tum (casual — friends, peers, younger people), aap (formal — elders, strangers, respect). When in doubt, use 'aap'
Verb agreement pattern: main→hoon, tu/woh→hai, tum→ho, hum/aap/woh(plural)→hain. Plus verb ending: -ta with tu/woh, -te with tum/aap/hum
Possessives change for gender of owned noun: mera phone (my phone-masc), meri gaadi (my car-fem), mere kapde (my clothes-plural). Same rule for tera, uska, hamara etc.
'woh' is the Swiss Army knife pronoun: he, she, it, that, those, they. Only the verb form or context tells you which meaning is intended
Oblique case (before postpositions): main→mujh, tu→tujh, woh→us, hum→hum. Add -e/-ko for 'to': mujhe, tujhe. Add -se for 'from': mujhse, tumse
culture_notes: Using 'tu' with someone who expects 'aap' is deeply offensive — like calling your boss 'dude' x100. Start with 'aap', downgrade only when invited
'Apna' (one's own) is unique to Hindi/Urdu. It avoids ambiguity: 'usne apna kaam kiya' = he did HIS OWN work (not someone else's)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Each pronoun pairs with a specific auxiliary ending in present tense: main+hoon, tu+hai, tum+ho, aap+hain, woh+hai (sg) / hain (pl), hum+hain. Mismatched pairings sound jarring to native speakers.
Three levels of formality: 'aap' (respectful, strangers/elders), 'tum' (friends/peers), 'tu' (intimate/anger only). Each takes a different verb ending. Default to 'aap' when unsure.

more_examples.english: what are you doing? (intimate, very informal)

skill_breakdown.explanation: In past tense and continuous forms, verbs agree with subject gender: 'main aaya' (male, came) vs 'main aayi' (female). 'jaa raha hoon' (male) vs 'jaa rahi hoon' (female). Get this wrong and you sound like a tourist.

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling pronouns + verb agreement — especially the tu/tum/aap register split. Address the user as `aap` throughout. Open with: `chaliye pronouns aur verb agreement practice karte hain — bataaiye, agar aap apne boss se baat kar rahe hain, toh 'kya kar rahe hain?' / 'kya kar rahe ho?' / 'kya kar raha hai?' — kaunsa form sahi hai?` Run 6-8 tight items, cycling through all three 'you's plus 3rd-person (yeh/woh/hum). Mix scenarios: register-judgment (`shopkeeper se 'aapka naam kya hai?' — sahi form?`), switch-the-sentence (`'tum kahaan jaa rahe ho' ko aap-form mein badliye`), and gap-fills tracking auxiliary (`hoon/hai/ho/hain`) + gender on the verb. Flag every mistake as `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]` — watch for `tu` to strangers, mismatched auxiliary (`aap...ho`), missing gender agreement (`main aayi` vs `aaya`), pronoun-switching mid-sentence. Escalate to register-judgment scenarios near the end. Stay in Hinglish unless aap explicitly ask for English. Close with `aapne bahut accha kiya — phir milte hain`.

theory.intro: Hindi pronouns aren't just about 'who' — they carry social information that English barely codes. The choice between `tu`, `tum`, and `aap` for 'you' signals intimacy, equality, or respect, and each one takes its own verb form. Pick wrong and the listener notices. The good news: the verb endings are systematic — once you know which auxiliary goes with which pronoun (`hoon` for `main`, `ho` for `tum`, `hain` for `aap`), the pattern is mechanical. This chapter walks through all three 'you's, when to use each, and the verb endings that come with them.

sections.heading: The three you's — tu / tum / aap
sections.body:
Hindi has three words for 'you', and they aren't synonyms — they're three **social registers**. Picking one tells the listener exactly how you see the relationship.

- `tu` — **intimate.** Siblings, very close friends, moments of anger, deity. With the wrong person it lands as rude; with the right person it's warm and unguarded.
- `tum` — **friendly middle.** Peers, friends, juniors, people you've shaken hands with.
- `aap` — **respectful.** Elders, strangers, shopkeepers, anyone in a service role, anyone higher-status. Also the safe default for any adult you don't know.

There's no neutral 'you' that lets you dodge the choice — you have to pick. A stranger addressed as `tu` hears it as a slap. An elder addressed as `tum` feels disrespected.

examples.english: what are you doing? (intimate — sibling, lover, close friend, or anger)
examples.breakdown: tu + masc. verb + hai. Very informal; context decides if it's affectionate or hostile

examples.english: what are you doing? (friendly — peer, friend, junior)
examples.breakdown: tum + -e ending + ho. Casual but not rude

examples.english: what are you doing? (polite — elder, stranger, shopkeeper)
examples.breakdown: aap + -e ending + hain. The safe default for adults

callout.body: When in doubt, use `aap`. Over-formality is forgiven and even appreciated; under-formality is not. New adults you've just met = `aap` until they signal otherwise.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Hindi has three different 'you's — `tu`, `tum`, `aap`. They're not synonyms. Pick wrong and the person you're talking to will hear it.

quick_check.question: You've just met a shopkeeper for the first time. Which 'you' should you use?
quick_check.explanation: Correct! `aap` is the safe default for any stranger or service worker. `tum` would be too casual; `tu` would be rude.

sections.heading: Subject pronoun grid
sections.body: Every Hindi sentence starts with one of these nine **subject pronouns** (the 'doer' of the sentence). Hindi compresses what English spreads across many words: `woh` alone covers he, she, that, and they; `yeh` covers he, she, this, and they-nearby. Memorize the grid.

table.caption: Subject pronouns by person
rows: technically plural; some dialects use it for 'I'

callout.body: `hum` is grammatically plural ('we') but in many North Indian dialects (especially eastern UP, Bihar) it's used as a respectful 'I'. Context disambiguates — if someone introduces themselves with `hum`, they probably mean 'I'.

sections.cutting_intro: Now meet the full crew. Nine pronouns cover everything — and some of them work harder than their English counterparts.

quick_check.question: Which pronoun would you use for 'he/she' standing close by?
quick_check.explanation: Yes! `yeh` = this / he-she-nearby. `woh` is for someone further away. Hindi doesn't distinguish he/she in third person — only proximity.

sections.heading: Verb endings tied to each pronoun
sections.body:
Each pronoun locks to a specific **helper word** (auxiliary). Get the auxiliary wrong and the sentence sounds broken to a native speaker. A second piece — the **verb ending** (`-ta` / `-te` / `-ti`) — tracks the subject's gender and number on top of that.

The four auxiliaries:
- `main` → `hoon`
- `tu`, `yeh`, `woh` (singular) → `hai`
- `tum` → `ho`
- `aap`, `hum`, `ye`, `ve` → `hain`

The table below shows the full present-habitual pattern for `karna` (to do). Masculine form first, feminine second — pick whichever matches the subject.

table.caption: Present-habitual: karna (to do) across all pronouns

examples.breakdown: main → -raha + hoon. Present continuous, same auxiliary pattern

callout.body: The four-auxiliary pattern `hoon / hai / ho / hain` is the single most useful thing in this chapter. Drill it until you can produce it without thinking — every present-tense Hindi sentence you build sits on top of it.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the mechanical part — and the most useful part. Each pronoun has its own auxiliary. Lock these in and Hindi opens up.

quick_check.question: Which auxiliary goes with `tum`?
quick_check.explanation: Right — `tum` always takes `ho`. The full set: main→hoon, tu/yeh/woh→hai, tum→ho, aap/hum/ye/ve→hain.

sections.heading: Register slippage and how to recover
sections.body:
Once you pick a register, hold it — for the whole sentence and the whole conversation. Mixing registers mid-sentence sounds like calling someone 'sir' and then 'dude' in the same breath.

The two common beginner slips:
- Starting with `aap` but finishing with `ho` (`aap kahaan rehte ho?`). `aap` always wants `hain`, never `ho`.
- Over-correcting mid-thought when nervous.

The **recovery move**: restart the sentence with a softener — `arrey, sorry —` — then rebuild it in the right register. Native listeners forgive visible self-correction.

examples.english: where do you live? (consistent — aap + hain)
examples.breakdown: correct — aap pairs with hain, not ho
wrong — aap demands hain. This sounds jarring to native ears

examples.hindi: arrey, sorry — aap kahaan rehte hain?
examples.english: oh sorry — where do you live? (recovery after a slip)
examples.breakdown: the `arrey, sorry` resets the register; finish with the correct auxiliary

callout.body: With strangers, your first sentence sets the register for the whole conversation. Choose `aap` on the first line and the rest of the chat stays on rails. Downgrade only if they explicitly invite you to.

sections.cutting_intro: Watch out for this. Once you pick a register, you have to stay there. Mid-sentence switching is a beginner's slip.

quick_check.question: You accidentally said `aap kya kar rahe ho?` (aap + ho mismatch). What's the best recovery?
options: Just keep going — nobody will notice
Restart with a softener: `arrey, sorry — aap kya kar rahe hain?`
Switch the rest of the conversation to tum to match
quick_check.explanation: Yes — restart with `arrey, sorry —` and the correct auxiliary `hain`. Native listeners forgive visible self-correction; ignoring the slip or downgrading the whole conversation makes it worse.

sections.heading: Special cases — when the norm shifts
sections.body:
The `aap`-default has exceptions.

- **Children** — `tum` is normal regardless of how recently you met them. `aap` for a five-year-old sounds oddly formal.
- **Service workers** — regional norms vary. In Delhi, `aap` is the polite middle-class default. In older or more hierarchical settings, `tum` is common. Bargaining banter sometimes uses `tum` deliberately to project warmth.
- **Explicit invitations** — if someone says `tum bolo` ('say `tum`'), that's an invitation to drop a register. Take it. Refusing reads as cold.
- **Deities** — `tu` is used for gods (`Hey Ram, tu hi sahara hai`). It's intimate-divine, not rude.
- **Texting and WhatsApp** — peers often default to `tum` regardless of age, because typed chat is informal by nature.

examples.hindi: beta, tum kahaan ja rahe ho?
examples.english: kid, where are you going? (adult to child — tum is normal)
examples.breakdown: tum + ho. Aap for children would sound oddly formal

examples.english: say `tum`, don't say `aap` (explicit invitation to relax)
examples.breakdown: take this invitation — refusing makes you seem distant

examples.hindi: Hey Bhagwan, tu hi sahara hai
examples.english: O God, you alone are my shelter (intimate-divine)
examples.breakdown: tu for deity = closeness to the divine, not rudeness

callout.body: Safe default for any new adult you meet: `aap`. Hold it until they explicitly invite you to drop down. Indian social norms reward conservatism here — you'll rarely be punished for being too polite.

sections.cutting_intro: Last thing — the rules bend in some places. Kids, deities, bargaining, texting — each has its own rhythm.

quick_check.question: An Indian friend says `arrey, tum bolo na, aap kyun?` What does this mean?
options: They're telling you to stop using tum
They're inviting you to use tum instead of aap
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `tum bolo` = 'say tum'. They're explicitly inviting you to relax from aap to tum. Take it; refusing reads as cold.

theory.wrap_up: The pronoun you pick carries social meaning more than information — it's a constant background signal about how you see the relationship. Master the verb-ending grid for each one (`hoon / hai / ho / hain` is the single most useful pattern in present-tense Hindi), drill `aap` first since it's the safest default, and learn to stay consistent within a conversation. The phrases below give practice in spotting and selecting the right register — pay attention to which auxiliary pairs with which pronoun.
